perturbation.original_text: For the term of 10 years, after receiving the INFORMATION and business data, the confidential information shall be kept safely, unless defined differently by a special agreement or it is proved to have been made publicly known.
perturbation.changed_text: For a term of 1 year, after receiving the INFORMATION and business data, the confidential information shall be kept safely, unless defined differently by a special agreement or it is proved to have been made publicly known.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term from 10 years to 1 year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Slovenian law, which recognizes longer protection periods for certain types of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovenian Protection of Undisclosed Know-How and Business Information (Trade Secrets) Act
perturbation.law_citation: Article 39 of the Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS Agreement)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 39 of the TRIPS agreement mandates protection against unfair competition, requiring protection of trade secrets for a sufficient period to prevent their unauthorized disclosure or use. Reducing the confidentiality term to just one year may not meet this requirement, particularly if the information retains significant commercial value beyond that period, effectively undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: WTO | intellectual property (TRIPS) - agreement text - standards WORLD TRADE ORGANIZATION Home | About WTO | News & events | Trade topics | WTO membership | Documents & resources | External relations Contact us | Site map | A-Z | Search español français home documents legal texts trips text URUGUAY ROUND AGREEMENT: TRIPS Part II — Standards concerning the availability, scope and use of Intellectual Property Rights Sections 5 and 6 Contents: PREAMBLE PART I General Provisions and Basic Principles PART II Standards Concerning the Availability, Scope and Use of Intellectual Property Rights 1. Copyright and Related Rights 2. Trademarks 3. Geographical Indications 4. Industrial Designs 5. Patents 6. Layout-Designs (Topographies) of Integrated Circuits 7. Protection of Undisclosed Information 8. Control of Anti-Competitive Practices in Contractual Licences PART III Enforcement of Intellectual Property Rights 1. General Obligations 2. Civil and Administrative Procedures and Remedies 3. Provisional Measures 4. Special Requirements Related to Border Measures 5. Criminal Procedures PART IV Acquisition and Maintenance of Intellectual Property Rights and Related Inter-Partes Procedures PART V Dispute Prevention and Settlement PART VI Transitional Arrangements PART VII Institutional Arrangements; Final Provisions back to top Section 5: patents Article 27 Patentable Subject Matter 1. Subject to the provisions of paragraphs 2 and 3, patents shall be available for any inventions, whether products or processes, in all fields of technology, provided that they are new, involve an inventive step and are capable of industrial application. (5) Subject to paragraph 4 of Article 65, paragraph 8 of Article 70 and paragraph 3 of this Article, patents shall be available and patent rights enjoyable without discrimination as to the place of invention, the field of technology and whether products are imported or locally produced. 2. Members may exclude from patentability inventions, the prevention within their territory of the commercial exploitation of which is necessary to protect ordre public or morality, including to protect human, animal or plant life or health or to avoid serious prejudice to the environment, provided that such exclusion is not made merely because the exploitation is prohibited by their law. 3. Members may also exclude from patentability: (a) diagnostic, therapeutic and surgical methods for the treatment of humans or animals; (b) plants and animals other than micro-organisms, and essentially biological processes for the production of plants or animals other than non-biological and microbiological processes. However, Members shall provide for the protection of plant varieties either by patents or by an effective sui generis system or by any combination thereof. The provisions of this subparagraph shall be reviewed four years after the date of entry into force of the WTO Agreement. Article 28 Rights Conferred 1. A patent shall confer on its owner the following exclusive rights: (a) where the subject matter of a patent is a product, to prevent third parties not having the owner’s consent from the acts of: making, using, offering for sale, selling, or importing (6) for these purposes that product; (b) where the subject matter of a patent is a process, to prevent third parties not having the owner’s consent from the act of using the process, and from the acts of: using, offering for sale, selling, or importing for these purposes at least the product obtained directly by that process. 2. Patent owners shall also have the right to assign, or transfer by succession, the patent and to conclude licensing contracts. Article 29 Conditions on Patent Applicants 1. Members shall require that an applicant for a patent shall disclose the invention in a manner sufficiently clear and complete for the invention to be carried out by a person skilled in the art and may require the applicant to indicate the best mode for carrying out the invention known to

perturbation.justification: The change from a 10-year to a 1-year confidentiality term directly contradicts the TRIPS agreement's mandate for protecting trade secrets against unfair competition, as stated in the Law Explanation. The TRIPS agreement requires protection for a sufficient period to prevent unauthorized disclosure or use. A one-year term, according to the Perturbation Explanation, may be insufficient, especially if the information retains significant commercial value beyond that period. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the TRIPS agreement under the WTO, specifically mentioning 'Protection of Undisclosed Information' as part of the standards concerning intellectual property rights. Reducing the term so drastically undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In this case, the recipient is to return all the material file of the INFORMATION and business data at once or at the latest in 7 (seven) days after receiving the request.
perturbation.changed_text: In this case, the recipient is to return all the material file of the INFORMATION and business data at once or at the latest in 30 (thirty) days after receiving the request.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the return period to 30 days could unduly delay the owner's access to critical confidential information, potentially conflicting with reasonable business practices and the owner's right to control its trade secrets under Slovenian law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovenian Obligations Code, general principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 5 of the Slovenian Obligations Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5 of the Slovenian Obligations Code emphasizes good faith and fair dealing in contractual relationships. Extending the return period to 30 days could be seen as acting in bad faith if it unreasonably delays the owner's access to its confidential information, particularly if the information is time-sensitive or critical to ongoing business operations, thereby undermining the principle of fairness in the contract.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a 7-day return period for confidential information, while the changed text extends it to 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests this extension could be seen as acting in bad faith under Article 5 of the Slovenian Obligations Code if it unreasonably delays the owner's access to its confidential information. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (returning information) is altered to potentially violate principles of fair dealing. Although scraped snippets do not directly confirm, the perturbation and law explanation combined with this inconsistency creates a legally questionable scenario.

perturbation.original_text: The agreement is valid when signed by both parties of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement is valid 30 days after being signed by both parties of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a 30-day waiting period for the agreement to become valid could delay the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the immediate need for confidentiality upon disclosure of sensitive data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovenian Contract Law principles of immediate effect upon agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 18 of the Slovenian Obligations Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 18 of the Slovenian Obligations Code states that a contract is concluded when the parties reach agreement. Imposing a 30-day waiting period contradicts this principle as it delays the agreement's effectiveness, creating a period where confidential information may be disclosed without contractual protection, thus undermining the immediate safeguarding of sensitive data intended by the NDA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day waiting period for the agreement to become valid. The Law Explanation states that Article 18 of the Slovenian Obligations Code dictates that a contract is concluded when the parties reach an agreement. The modified text contradicts this by delaying the agreement's effectiveness, potentially leading to unprotected disclosure of confidential information during the 30-day period. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this conflict, noting that the delay undermines the immediate need for confidentiality intended by the NDA. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly support this, the provided explanations indicate a contradiction with legal principles regarding contract validity upon agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties agree that eventual disputes arising from this contract are to be solved in agreement. If this is not possible, the Court of Law related to the owner, i.e. Domel, is competent to deal with the dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties agree that eventual disputes arising from this contract are to be solved in agreement. If this is not possible, the Court of Arbitration in Zurich, Switzerland, shall have exclusive jurisdiction over any dispute arising out of or relating to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Designating the Court of Arbitration in Zurich, Switzerland, for dispute resolution might contradict Slovenian procedural law if the agreement's subject matter and parties are primarily located in Slovenia, potentially infringing on mandatory jurisdiction rules.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovenian Law on Private International Law (rules on jurisdiction).
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of the Lugano Convention deals with the jurisdiction over consumer contracts. Although not directly a consumer contract, the principle of ensuring access to justice in the jurisdiction where the parties are established is relevant. If the agreement is closely connected to Slovenia, mandating arbitration in Zurich could be seen as an attempt to circumvent Slovenian courts and potentially disadvantage the supplier, especially if they are a smaller entity, thereby potentially contravening the spirit of ensuring fair access to justice.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the Court of Law related to the owner (Domel) for dispute resolution. The changed text designates the Court of Arbitration in Zurich, Switzerland, for dispute resolution. This is an inconsistency because it changes the specified jurisdiction for dispute resolution from a local court to a foreign arbitration court, potentially contradicting procedural law as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. The Perturbation explanation states that designating a court in Zurich might contradict Slovenian procedural law if the agreement is based in Slovenia. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where a deadline or requirement violates federal or state law.
